scope.question: Which of merge sort, quicksort, and radix sort is fastest on average across 10 trials on random integer arrays of size 10K, 100K, and 1M in pure Python?
in_scope: Pure Python implementations of merge sort, quicksort, and radix sort
Random integer arrays of size 10K, 100K, and 1M
10 timing trials per (algorithm, array size) combination
Correctness verification: all three algorithms produce identical sorted output
out_of_scope: In-place variants, stability analysis, or memory profiling
unresolved_questions: Integer range for random arrays not yet specified — need to choose (affects radix sort digit count and may bias results)
No external benchmark reference selected — timing baseline established from scratch

user_asserted_anchors: Results must respect theoretical complexity: O(n log n) for merge sort and quicksort, O(n) for radix sort — gross violations indicate buggy implementations
context_gaps: Integer range for random arrays not yet specified
No external benchmark reference — timing baseline established from scratch

formulations: Pure Python implementations with wall-clock timing via time.perf_counter, 10 trials per combination
allowed_fit_families: mean and stddev summary statistics over 10 trials
stop_and_rethink_conditions: Any algorithm produces unsorted output on any trial
Timing ratio N=1M/N=10K for merge sort or quicksort falls outside [20, 500]
Radix sort is slower than merge sort at N=1M by more than 5x

observables.name: Wall-clock time per algorithm per array size
observables.definition: Mean and standard deviation of wall-clock execution time in seconds over 10 trials for each of 9 combinations: 3 algorithms x 3 array sizes

observables.name: Empirical complexity scaling ratio
observables.definition: Ratio of mean time at N=1M to mean time at N=10K for each algorithm, used to verify O(n log n) vs O(n) scaling

claims.statement: One algorithm achieves the lowest mean wall-clock time averaged across all three array sizes, determined from 10 trials per combination with correctness verified

deliverables.description: Timing table: mean and stddev wall-clock seconds for each of 9 (algorithm, array size) combinations across 10 trials

acceptance_tests.subject: claim-winner
acceptance_tests.procedure: On each trial, verify all three algorithms produce output identical to sorted() on the same input array
acceptance_tests.pass_condition: Zero correctness failures across all 30 trial runs

acceptance_tests.subject: claim-winner
acceptance_tests.procedure: Compute time ratio N=1M/N=10K for each algorithm. Merge sort and quicksort expected in [20, 500]; radix sort expected in [20, 300].
acceptance_tests.pass_condition: All three algorithms within expected scaling bands

acceptance_tests.subject: deliv-table
acceptance_tests.procedure: Verify timing table contains exactly 9 rows (3 algorithms x 3 sizes) with positive mean and non-negative stddev
acceptance_tests.pass_condition: Table has 9 rows with all mean values positive and all stddev values non-negative

forbidden_proxies.subject: claim-winner
forbidden_proxies.proxy: Claiming a winner based on theoretical O() complexity alone without measured wall-clock data
forbidden_proxies.reason: Pure Python constant factors dominate at these sizes; O(n) radix sort is not guaranteed to win in practice

forbidden_proxies.subject: claim-winner
forbidden_proxies.proxy: Reporting a single-trial timing as the representative result without mean and stddev over 10 trials
forbidden_proxies.reason: Single trials are noisy; 10-trial statistics are required for a credible claim

weakest_anchors: Timing results may not reproduce across machines with different Python versions, CPU characteristics, or OS scheduling
unvalidated_assumptions: Integer range for random arrays not yet chosen — affects radix sort digit count and may bias relative performance
10 trials assumed sufficient to estimate mean reliably; variance under this sample size is unvalidated
competing_explanations: Quicksort may outperform radix sort in pure Python despite worse asymptotic complexity due to better cache behavior and lower per-element overhead
disconfirming_observations: If merge sort or quicksort time ratio (N=1M/N=10K) falls outside [20, 500], the implementation is likely buggy
If radix sort is more than 5x slower than merge sort at N=1M, the radix sort implementation has excessive Python overhead

open_questions: Integer range for random arrays not yet specified — need to choose (affects radix sort digit count and may bias results)
No external benchmark reference selected — timing baseline established from scratch
